title: Maximum Number of Coins After K Transmutations

statement.markdown: You are given an array of integers `coins` representing the number of coins you have of each denomination, and an integer `k` representing the number of transmutations you can perform. In one transmutation, you can transform a coin of a smaller denomination into a coin of a larger denomination. Your goal is to maximize the number of coins you have after k transmutations. For example, if you have coins of denominations [1, 5, 10] and you can perform 3 transmutations, you can transform the 5 and 10 coins into 10 coins, resulting in a total of 20 coins.
statement.inputFormat:
Input will be in the following format: "coins k"
coins: space-separated list of coin denominations, k: number of transmutations

Example: "1 5 10 3"
statement.outputFormat: Output the maximum number of coins that can be collected after k transmutations.
statement.notes: : Time limit: 1 second, Memory limit: 256 MB

examples.explanation: Transform 1 and 5 coins into 10 coins to get 20 coins.
Transform 2 coins into 5 coins to get 8 coins.

hints.text: Sort the coins array in ascending order before processing

editorial.title: Maximum Number of Coins After K Transmutations
editorial.intuition: The greedy approach is to always transform coins of smaller denominations into coins of larger denominations. This will ensure that we maximize the number of coins after k transmutations.
editorial.bruteForce: We can use a brute force approach by iterating over all possible combinations of coin transformations and checking if each transformation results in the maximum number of coins after k transmutations.
editorial.optimalSolution: To solve this problem efficiently, we can use a greedy approach. We first sort the coins array in ascending order. Then, we initialize two pointers: `start` at the first index of the coins array and `end` at the last index of the coins array. We also initialize a variable `ans` to keep track of the maximum number of coins after k transmutations.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The greedy approach works because it always chooses the option that maximizes the number of coins after k transmutations.
editorial.dryRun: Let's work through an example: coins = [1, 5, 10], k = 3. We first sort the coins array to get [1, 5, 10]. We initialize two pointers: start = 0 and end = 2. We also initialize a variable ans = 0.
commonMistakes: Not sorting the coins array before processing
Not using a greedy approach to maximize the number of coins after k transmutations
